description: A benzodiazepine (brand Valium) for anxiety, seizures, muscle spasm, and alcohol withdrawal. It enhances the calming neurotransmitter GABA and is long-acting, partly because of its active metabolite.

halfLife.conditions: terminal (β-phase) elimination half-life, young healthy adults; ~20 h at age 20 rising with age (FDA states up to ~48 h). This is the terminal slope carried for the caption/provenance rows; the engine recomputes β from the disposition2c micro-parameters (round-trips to ~33 h).

vd.conditions: 'In young healthy males, the volume of distribution at steady-state is 0.8 to 1.0 L/kg' (FDA/DailyMed Valium label) — the range is the label's own, for the same young-adult population this file models. The derived Vc + Vp = 0.25 + 0.7474 = 1.00 L/kg reproduces the top of it. NOTE what that cross-check does and does not prove: it constrains CL and β (Vβ = CL/β ≈ 1.11 L/kg) and is nearly INDEPENDENT of Vc (any Vc in 0.2–0.4 L/kg lands Vss near ~1), so it does NOT pin the distribution-peak height C(0) = Dose/Vc. Vc is instead pinned by the Klotz V1-vs-age regression at the file's anchor age (see disposition2c.centralVd). Scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject at derivation time.

clearance.conditions: total plasma clearance 0.39 mL/min/kg in young healthy males (Greenblatt 1980), expressed here as 0.0234 L/h/kg (≈1.64 L/h ≈ 27 mL/min for the 70 kg reference subject — within the FDA 'clearance of 20–30 mL/min in young adults'). Sets AUC = Dose/CL.

centralVd.conditions: central (initial distribution) volume V1, EVALUATED FROM Klotz 1975's published age regression at this file's 28-year anchor age (see notes, POPULATION): Table II gives 'VI/kg vs. age — slope 0.0061, intercept 0.08' (n=16, r=+0.70, P=0.003), so V1 = 0.0061·28 + 0.08 = 0.2508 → 0.25 L/kg. derived: true because it is computed from a regression, not read off as a number — Klotz reports no single young-adult V1. This value alone sets the early peak C(0) = Dose/Vc, and V1 is *defined* as Dose/C(0), so anchoring it here is what makes the distribution-phase amplitude and the oral peak defensible rather than illustrative. (It was previously 0.3 L/kg, a 'customary young-end' value carrying no source; 0.3 is the regression's value at age ~36, which contradicted the 28-year half-life and clearance — see notes.)

interCompartmentalClearance.conditions: DERIVED, not reported by any single source: Q = k12·Vc, computed offline from CL (Greenblatt), Vc (Klotz's V1 regression at age 28), distribution t½(α)≈1 h (CHEMM/FDA) and terminal t½(β)≈33 h (Klotz). sourceRef names the primary structural source (Klotz's 2-comp model); the full multi-source arithmetic is in notes. ≈8.14 L/h at 70 kg.

peripheralVd.conditions: DERIVED, not reported by any single source: Vp = Vc·k12/k21, computed offline from CL (Greenblatt), Vc (Klotz's V1 regression at age 28), t½(α) (CHEMM/FDA) and t½(β) (Klotz). sourceRef names the primary structural source; full multi-source arithmetic in notes. ≈52.3 L at 70 kg; Vc + Vp = 1.00 L/kg sits at the top of the FDA label's 0.8–1.0 L/kg Vd(ss) (a CL/β cross-check, not a Vc check — see disposition.vd). Residual: this construction's k12/k21 = 2.99 runs ~9% above the 2.75 Klotz's Table II reports (age-independent, P=0.848), because α is taken from CHEMM/FDA (~1 h) rather than from Klotz — see notes, RESIDUAL.

F.conditions: BIOAVAILABILITY (not absorption): 'the bioavailability of the oral drug was only about 75%' (Klotz 1975), measured as oral-vs-intravenous AUC in the same study, same subjects, same assay as this file's disposition — which is why it is preferred over a cross-population figure. This does NOT contradict the FDA label's 'After oral administration >90% of diazepam is absorbed': absorption (fraction reaching the portal circulation) and bioavailability (fraction reaching the systemic circulation) are different quantities, and the two reconcile with ~15–20% lost to hepatic first pass. The engine's F is bioavailability, so 0.75 is the correct quantity to store.

tmax.conditions: 'After oral administration of 10 mg diazepam, absorption was rapid and peak plasma levels of diazepam (221-440 ng/ml) were observed 1 h after ingestion' (Klotz 1975). ka is derived from this Tmax at derivation time through the two-compartment peak solve (kaFromTmax2c), giving ka ≈ 1.76 /h (absorption t½ ≈ 24 min). Corroborated across populations: the FDA label reports peak plasma concentrations at 1–1.5 h (range 0.25–2.5 h; 1.25 h fasting), and Locniskar 1989 measured Tmax 0.98 h for brand Valium in 26 healthy males. CAVEAT — Klotz found absorption to be BIPHASIC ('the rate of absorption of oral diazepam was initially rapid, it was followed by a slower rate of absorption', producing an 'absorption nose'); a single first-order ka cannot represent that, so the modelled curve decays somewhat faster after the peak than the real one. This is a deliberate, documented simplification, not an oversight.

metabolites.name: Nordiazepam (N-desmethyldiazepam)
metabolites.description: The major active metabolite of diazepam. It is eliminated much more slowly than the parent (half-life up to ~100 h), so on the chart it builds up and overtakes diazepam late in the course.

fractionFormed.conditions: ≈50–60% of a diazepam dose is N-demethylated to nordiazepam (IARC monograph, attributing Bertilsson et al. 1990); nordiazepam is the major circulating metabolite. The minor pathways (3-hydroxylation to temazepam, then oxazepam) are not modelled. Point value 55%.

vd.conditions: DERIVED to be consistent with the metabolite's own clearance and half-life: Vd_m = CL_m·t½_m/ln2 = 0.678 L/h × 60 h / ln2 ≈ 58.7 L ≈ 0.84 L/kg, from CL_m 11.3 mL/min (CHEMM) and t½_m 60 h. This makes nordiazepam exposure effectively clearance-defined (AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m); it is NOT an independently measured volume. Nordiazepam is itself multi-compartment in reality — modelled here as one-compartment (the metabolite engine's scope).

halfLife.conditions: terminal elimination half-life; FDA states 'up to 100 h', IARC/other sources report ~40–120 h. Point value 60 h. Nordiazepam is eliminated ~3× slower than diazepam and accumulates on repeated dosing.

metabolites.notes:
Nordiazepam (nordazepam / N-desmethyldiazepam) is the principal active metabolite of diazepam. It is COMPUTED end-to-end by the metabolite engine (formation rate = the parent's disposition modes; own 1-comp elimination) and DRAWN as a dashed chart line for an IV-bolus parent (the metabolite <Line> rows landed for both models; see handoff §12). fm is the one-time blocker that the IARC 50–60%-demethylated figure resolves; the metabolite Vd is derived from its clearance and half-life rather than measured.

ORAL (2026-07-17). The line is now drawn on the oral route too, from SYSTEMIC formation only — cleared on Klotz 1975's direct observation that oral desmethyldiazepam's profile 'was comparable to that observed after intravenous diazepam administration'. No ffp term (nordiazepam peaks at 24–48 h vs the parent's 1 h, failing the first-pass timing screen), so with F = 0.75 the oral line sits ~25% below the IV one. See the parent's ROUTES notes for the full reasoning.

fda_label.title: VALIUM (diazepam) tablets — FDA Structured Product Label, clinical pharmacology (DailyMed setid 554baee5): '>90% of diazepam is absorbed' orally with peak plasma concentrations at '1 – 1.5 hours with a range of 0.25 to 2.5 hours' (2.5 h fed vs 1.25 h fasting; food cuts Cmax ~20% and AUC ~27%); initial distribution phase 'half-life of approximately 1 hour, although it may range up to >3 hours'; terminal half-life up to 48 h; 'In young healthy males, the volume of distribution at steady-state is 0.8 to 1.0 L/kg'; 'The clearance of diazepam is 20 to 30 mL/min in young adults'; plasma protein binding 98%; N-desmethyldiazepam active metabolite, terminal half-life up to 100 h; CYP3A4/2C19 metabolism

locniskar_1989.title: Locniskar A, Greenblatt DJ, Harmatz JS, Shader RI. Bioinequivalence of a generic brand of diazepam. Biopharm Drug Dispos. 1989 Nov-Dec;10(6):597–605 (crossover in 26 healthy male volunteers, single 10 mg dose; brand Valium reference Cmax 394 ng/mL at Tmax 0.98 h, AUC 7552 ng·mL⁻¹·h; generic NeoCalme Cmax 247 ng/mL at Tmax 1.62 h). Used only as an INDEPENDENT magnitude corroboration of the oral peak — no parameter is fitted to it.

greenblatt_1980.title: Greenblatt DJ, Allen MD, Harmatz JS, Shader RI. Diazepam disposition determinants. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 1980;27(3):301–312 (two-compartment analysis; clearance ~0.39 mL/min/kg in young males)

klotz_1975.title: Klotz U, Avant GR, Hoyumpa A, Schenker S, Wilkinson GR. The effects of age and liver disease on the disposition and elimination of diazepam in adult man. J Clin Invest. 1975;55(2):347–359. Diazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or 10 mg ORALLY to 33 normal volunteers aged 15–82; IV data analysed 'according to the two-compartment open system'. Table II least-squares regressions vs age: V1/kg slope 0.0061 intercept 0.08 (n=16, r=+0.70, P=0.003); Vd(ss)/kg slope 0.0172 intercept 0.42; t½(β) nonsmokers slope 1.094 intercept 2.26; k12/k21 vs age NOT significant (P=0.848, ≈2.75); clearance vs age slope −0.186 intercept 32.52 (P=0.089, i.e. no age dependence), 'between 20 and 32 ml/min'. Vd(ss) = V1·[1 + k12/k21]. ORAL arm: 'peak plasma levels of diazepam (221-440 ng/ml) were observed 1 h after ingestion'; absorption biphasic ('initially rapid … followed by a slower rate of absorption', an 'absorption nose'); 'the bioavailability of the oral drug was only about 75%'; desmethyldiazepam 'plasma level/time profile … was comparable to that observed after intravenous diazepam administration', rising to 'a shallow maximum between 24 and 48 h'. Full text read via the JCI open-access PDF (jci.org/articles/view/107938).

iarc_monograph.title: IARC Monographs Vol. 66 — Diazepam, ADME section (states ~50–60% of diazepam is demethylated to N-desmethyldiazepam, citing Bertilsson et al. 1990). NCBI Bookshelf NBK423855

chemm.title: CHEMM (Chemical Hazards Emergency Medical Management, HHS) — Diazepam Medical Countermeasures Database: distribution half-life 0.3–1.3 h; N-desmethyldiazepam clearance 11.3 ± 3.1 mL/min

metabolism: Diazepam is broken down by the liver (CYP3A4 and CYP2C19) mainly to nordiazepam (N-desmethyldiazepam), a long-lived active metabolite eliminated about three times more slowly than the parent. Nordiazepam accumulates on repeated dosing and is a major reason diazepam is so long-acting. Minor further metabolites (temazepam, oxazepam) are not modelled here.
notes:
FIRST real two-compartment compound (handoff §12) and the first shipped parent→metabolite pair. Diazepam has a pronounced distribution phase (fast α, t½ ~1 h) then a long terminal phase (β, t½ ~20–48 h) — the biphasic structure that was the decisive disqualifier under the one-compartment metabolite spike (docs/DATA_GUIDE.md rejection log) and is exactly what the 2-comp engine now represents.

POPULATION — ANCHORED TO A SINGLE AGE, 28 YEARS. Every disposition input is a young-healthy-adult value kept to ONE population, because diazepam kinetics drift strongly with age (terminal t½ ~20 h at 20 y to ~90 h at 80 y) and mixing a young clearance with an older half-life breaks the compartmental algebra. Klotz 1975 lets that be stated precisely rather than loosely, because Table II publishes each parameter as a regression ON AGE — so an age, not a vibe, selects the set. This file's two long-standing values each imply the SAME age: t½(β) = 33 h inverts Klotz's t½(β) = 1.094·age + 2.26 to age ≈ 28, and CL = 27.3 mL/min matches Klotz's clearance regression 32.52 − 0.186·age at age ≈ 28 (and Greenblatt's independently-measured 0.39 mL/min/kg in young males, whose group was aged 21–37). So 28 y is not a new assumption imported for the oral route — it is the age the file already encoded. Vc was the one parameter off that anchor: the former 0.3 L/kg is Klotz's V1 regression at age ~36, an inconsistency invisible while only IV shipped and the peak was labelled illustrative. Adding oral made Vc load-bearing (it sets the oral peak) and forced the correction to 0.25 L/kg = V1 at age 28.

DISPOSITION2C — what is sourced vs derived. The schema stores CL/Vc/Q/Vp, but Q and Vp are rarely reported directly, so only CL and Vc come from sources and Q, Vp are computed offline (derived: true) from four macro-observables: CL = 0.39 mL/min/kg (Greenblatt 1980, young males), Vc = V1 = 0.25 L/kg (Klotz Table II regression V1/kg = 0.0061·age + 0.08 at age 28 → 0.2508), distribution t½(α) ~1 h (CHEMM/FDA) and terminal t½(β) ~33 h (Klotz, age 28). Working in per-kg units: k10 = CL/Vc = 0.0936/h; α = ln2/1 = 0.6931/h, β = ln2/33 = 0.02100/h; k21 = α·β/k10 = 0.1556/h; k12 = (α+β) − k10 − k21 = 0.4650/h; Q = k12·Vc = 0.1163 L/h/kg; Vp = Q/k21 = 0.7474 L/kg. The constraint β < k10 < α holds (0.0210 < 0.0936 < 0.6931), so k12, k21 are positive. The engine round-trips these stored micro-parameters back to α t½ = 1.00 h and β t½ = 33.0 h.

WHAT THE Vss CROSS-CHECK DOES AND DOES NOT PROVE. Vc + Vp = 1.00 L/kg lands at the top of the FDA label's 0.8–1.0 L/kg Vd(ss), but that agreement is essentially a check on CL and β, not on Vc: Vss = CL(α+β)/(αβ) − CL²/(αβ·Vc), which for these CL, α, β lands ~0.96–1.05 L/kg across the whole plausible Vc range 0.2–0.4 L/kg. So the cross-check validates the terminal disposition (Vβ = CL/β ≈ 1.11 L/kg) but is nearly BLIND to Vc — it cannot be used to justify a central volume. What pins Vc is Klotz's V1-vs-age regression at the anchor age, and V1 is *defined* as Dose/C(0), so it is the right instrument for exactly the quantity Vc controls. That is what upgraded the early peak from illustrative to defensible, and it is independently corroborated by the oral peak (below) — a landmark Vc alone governs.

RESIDUAL — the honest ~10% left over. This is still a MULTI-SOURCE construction, and it does not close perfectly. Taking α from CHEMM/FDA (~1 h) rather than from Klotz makes the derived k12/k21 = 2.99, ~9% above the 2.75 Klotz's Table II reports (age-independent, P=0.848); correspondingly Vss = 1.00 L/kg against the 0.90 L/kg Klotz's own Vd(ss) regression gives at age 28. Using Klotz's k12/k21 = 2.75 throughout instead would imply α t½ ≈ 1.5 h — still inside the FDA label's 'approximately 1 hour, although it may range up to >3 hours', but contradicting CHEMM's 0.3–1.3 h and rewriting the caption's α. The ~10% was accepted and recorded rather than hidden; α, not Vc, is now the softest input in the set.

METABOLITE (nordiazepam). fm was the one-time blocker in the rejection log (uncited fraction converted) — resolved by the IARC monograph's '~50–60% of diazepam is demethylated' (attributing Bertilsson et al. 1990), stored as 55% (range 50–60). The metabolite Vd is not measured; it is derived to be consistent with nordiazepam's own clearance (11.3 mL/min, CHEMM) and half-life (60 h) via Vd_m = CL_m·t½_m/ln2 ≈ 0.84 L/kg, which makes its exposure clearance-defined (AUC_m = fm·Dose/CL_m). Nordiazepam is COMPUTED end-to-end and now RENDERS as a dashed metabolite line on the IV-bolus chart (handoff §12).

ROUTES. Oral and IV bolus are marked available; iv_infusion is offered as inferred (available: false) since diazepam is given by IV push, not continuous infusion (Valium injectable is a real slow-IV product).

ORAL — SHIPPED 2026-07-17 (was deferred pending 'a defensible oral ka/Tmax in this one young-healthy-adult population'). Klotz 1975 supplies exactly that, and from the SAME study and population as the disposition: Tmax ~1 h and F ~75%. Both quantities are named carefully. F is a true BIOAVAILABILITY (oral-vs-IV AUC in the same subjects), which is why it is preferred over cross-population figures; the FDA label's '>90% of diazepam is absorbed' is a DIFFERENT quantity (absorption, not systemic availability) and the two reconcile with ~15–20% first-pass loss — they were not averaged. ka is not stored: it is derived from Tmax at derivation time via the 2-comp peak solve (kaFromTmax2c), giving ka ≈ 1.76 /h (absorption t½ ≈ 24 min).

ORAL MAGNITUDE CHECK (the gate that matters — npm test proves structure, never magnitude). Built against the engine curve: 10 mg oral → Cmax 255 ng/mL at Tmax 1.00 h, INSIDE Klotz's own reported oral peak range of 221–440 ng/mL at 1 h — the same paper, same population, so the check is not cross-population. Independently corroborated by Locniskar 1989 (brand Valium, 10 mg, 26 healthy males): Tmax 0.98 h, Cmax 394 ng/mL — near the top of Klotz's spread, and the model runs 35% under it. That gap is inter-individual spread, NOT a model failure, and Vc was deliberately NOT tuned to close it: Vc is fixed by Klotz's V1 regression at the anchor age, so the peak remains an independent prediction rather than a fit. (Fitting Vc to Locniskar's 394 would have forced Vc ≈ 0.20 L/kg = age 20, which drags t½(β) to ~24 h and contradicts the file's own 33 h — a two-age composite, precisely what POPULATION above forbids.) The AUC story is a genuine SOURCE DISAGREEMENT, worth seeing rather than smoothing: at F = 0.75 this model gives AUC ≈ 4580 ng·mL⁻¹·h against Locniskar's 7552, because AUC = F·D/CL depends only on CL and F, and Locniskar's AUC implies a lower CL than Greenblatt's 27.3 mL/min. The FDA label's own '20 to 30 mL/min' spans both readings.

ORAL — TWO DOCUMENTED SIMPLIFICATIONS. (1) Absorption is modelled as a SINGLE first-order ka, but Klotz observed it to be biphasic — 'initially rapid … followed by a slower rate of absorption', an 'absorption nose'. One ka cannot render that, so the modelled curve decays a little faster after the peak than the real one. (2) The nordiazepam line is drawn on the oral route from SYSTEMIC formation only. This clears the oseltamivir/nicotine criterion (do not draw a metabolite whose formation is largely pre-systemic) on Klotz's direct observation that oral desmethyldiazepam's 'plasma level/time profile was comparable to that observed after intravenous diazepam administration'. No firstPassFraction (ffp) term is used: ffp forms the metabolite at the PARENT's ka, which requires metabolite Tmax ≈ parent Tmax, and nordiazepam peaks at 24–48 h against the parent's 1 h — it fails the first-pass timing screen outright. The cost is bounded and directional: because F = 0.75 routes only 75% of an oral dose to systemic formation, the oral nordiazepam line sits ~25% below the IV one, whereas Klotz called the two comparable — i.e. this line UNDER-draws the oral metabolite by roughly the first-pass fraction. Klotz made 'no attempt … to analyze pharmacokinetically' the metabolite, so 'comparable' is a qualitative read that a 25% offset would not have disturbed.

Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
